_comment: Curated extraction of data/documentary-form-atlas.md. Tags are axis sub-category numbers (e.g. 1.3) or special tag keys (unclassified). kind: FILM | SERIES | BODY_OF_WORK | INSTITUTION. Entries with noTmdb: true are created without TMDB matching. search overrides the TMDB query when the display title differs from the TMDB title.

films.title: Senna
films.formalNote: Defined the modern archival feature: no talking heads, present-tense storytelling built purely from race footage, home video, and broadcast archive.

films.title: Amy
films.formalNote: Kapadia's archival formula applied to Amy Winehouse. The archival relationship at its most ethically fraught: friends who supplied footage later objected to the film's framing.

films.title: Diego Maradona
films.formalNote: Third of Kapadia's archival-only trilogy: no talking heads, present-tense storytelling built from archive.

films.title: Apollo 11
films.formalNote: Assembled from rediscovered 65mm NASA archive and 11,000 hours of audio. No narration, no interviews, no reenactment. The archive quality is the spectacle.

films.title: They Shall Not Grow Old
films.formalNote: Restoration as technique: WWI footage retimed, colorized, with forensic lip-readers reconstructing dialogue. Worth studying for the ethics of enhancement.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.formalNote: Built around a single unseen archive (1969 Harlem Cultural Festival tapes) with present-day reaction interviews woven in. The rediscovery is part of the narrative.

films.title: The Velvet Underground
films.formalNote: Archival plus persistent split-screen borrowed from Warhol's Exploding Plastic Inevitable, so the form of the film mimics the aesthetic of its subject.

films.title: Waltz with Bashir
films.formalNote: Animated interviews about the 1982 Lebanon war. Animation represents the unreliability of traumatic memory; the switch to real archive in the final seconds is the whole argument.

films.title: Flee
films.formalNote: Animation protects the anonymity of an Afghan refugee while allowing full emotional intimacy. First film Oscar-nominated for documentary, animated feature, and international feature simultaneously.

films.title: Tower
films.formalNote: Rotoscoped reenactments of the 1966 UT Austin shooting, blended with archive. Rotoscope lets survivors appear as their younger selves.

films.title: Fire of Love
films.formalNote: Primarily archival (the Kraffts' own volcano footage) but uses paper-cutout animation for gaps. A good study in mixing archival-only and animation.

films.title: Midnight Traveler
films.formalNote: An Afghan family's three-year refugee journey filmed entirely on three mobile phones by the family themselves. The constraint is total and the intimacy is unmatched.

films.title: For Sama
films.formalNote: Five years of self-shot footage in besieged Aleppo, framed as a letter to the filmmaker's daughter. First-person capture plus epistolary structure.

films.title: 20 Days in Mariupol
films.formalNote: A news journalist's own footage recut as cinema; the story includes the mechanics of getting the footage out.

films.title: Born into Brothels
films.formalNote: Children given still cameras; their photographs become both the method and a plot engine. The canonical participatory-media example, also much critiqued: participation as method but not as authorship.

films.title: The Territory
films.formalNote: Mid-production, the Uru-eu-wau-wau community took over their own cinematography during COVID and received co-production credit. A modern model for shared authorship.

films.title: Leviathan
films.formalNote: Dozens of GoPros attached to a fishing trawler, to fishermen, tossed into fish bins and underwater. No interviews, no context, pure sensation. Launched the Sensory Ethnography Lab school.

films.title: Gunda
films.formalNote: Black and white, animal-height cameras, no music, no narration, no humans. The technique forces you to watch a pig as a protagonist.

films.title: Cow
films.formalNote: Animal-height commitment applied to a single dairy cow over years, with a harsher industrial framing.

films.title: Chasing Ice
films.formalNote: Time-lapse rigs left on glaciers for years. Building and maintaining the camera systems is the narrative spine, and the resulting images are the evidence.

films.title: Present.Perfect.
films.formalNote: Assembled entirely from Chinese livestream broadcasts. The director never filmed anything; she watched and curated hundreds of hours of streams.

films.title: Put Your Soul on Your Hand and Walk
films.formalNote: Constructed from a year of video calls with Gaza photojournalist Fatma Hassona. The low-res call footage is the point: distance, fragility of connection, and what a phone link can and cannot carry.

films.title: Transformers: The Premake
films.formalNote: A desktop documentary made inside a computer screen, analyzing a blockbuster through YouTube clips, maps, and browser windows. Foundational for the desktop-doc form.

films.title: Welcome to Chechnya
films.formalNote: AI face-swapping (digital veils) protects LGBTQ+ activists while preserving their real facial emotion. The moment a subject removes the veil on screen is a formal event in the story.

films.title: Another Body
films.formalNote: A deepfake-abuse victim is herself protected by a consensual deepfake face. The technique mirrors the subject perfectly.

films.title: Roadrunner
films.formalNote: Used AI-synthesized Anthony Bourdain voice for a few lines without disclosure. The cautionary ethics case for voice synthesis.

search.query: Roadrunner: A Film About Anthony Bourdain

films.title: Cave of Forgotten Dreams
films.formalNote: 3D justified by subject: the Chauvet cave paintings were made on curved rock, and depth is essential to seeing them as their makers did.

films.title: Pina
films.formalNote: 3D for dance, capturing choreography's spatial dimension that flat film loses.

films.title: 2000 Meters to Andriivka
films.formalNote: Ukrainian assault filmed largely through soldiers' bodycams and drone feeds, stitched into near-continuous forward movement. Operational military footage as first-person cinema.

films.title: Eno
films.formalNote: Software generates a different cut at every screening; no two audiences see the same film. Form matches Eno's generative music philosophy. The defining example of generative documentary.

films.title: The Fog of War
films.formalNote: The Interrotron: a modified teleprompter rig that lets the subject look directly into the lens while seeing Morris' face. Direct eye contact with the audience.

films.title: Beckham
films.formalNote: A replay screen beside the lens shows subjects footage of decisive moments while a tight lens captures their unguarded reaction. The interview becomes about watching someone remember.

films.title: Look into My Eyes
films.formalNote: Psychic readings filmed as intimate two-person sessions in natural light, with no captions, narration, or music. The interview is a session between subjects, not between subject and filmmaker.

films.title: Shoah
films.formalNote: Nine and a half hours, zero archival footage. Only present-day testimony and the landscapes where the Holocaust happened. Duration as moral refusal of summary.

films.title: The Look of Silence
films.formalNote: An optometrist whose brother was murdered in the 1965 Indonesian genocide interviews the killers while fitting them for glasses. The eye-test frame gives the confrontation a devastating formal logic.

films.title: The Act of Killing
films.formalNote: Death squad leaders restage their killings in the film genres of their choice. The gap between their self-image and their acts becomes visible on camera. Arguably the most influential formal invention in 21st century documentary.

films.title: Procession
films.formalNote: Six survivors of clergy abuse write and direct their own reenactment scenes, with a drama therapist involved throughout. Direction as therapy, therapy as film.

films.title: Casting JonBenet
films.formalNote: Local actors audition for roles in a film that never gets made. Audition-room monologues expose a community's theories, projections, and private griefs. The casting session replaces the interview entirely.

films.title: Bisbee '17
films.formalNote: An entire town restages a 1917 mass deportation of miners. Civic-scale reenactment as collective interview.

films.title: The Arbor
films.formalNote: Actors lip-sync precisely to real recorded interviews about playwright Andrea Dunbar. The visible seam between real voice and performing body keeps you constantly aware of mediation.

films.title: Frederick Wiseman: institutional portraits
films.formalNote: From Titicut Follies (1967) through Menus-Plaisirs (2023): six decades of institutional portraits with no interviews, narration, captions, or music. Structure emerges purely in the edit. Watch any one.

films.title: National Gallery
films.formalNote: A representative late Wiseman: an institution observed with no interviews, narration, captions, or music.

films.title: Titicut Follies
films.formalNote: The start of Wiseman's six-decade project of institutional portraits without interviews or narration.

films.title: Menus-Plaisirs - Les Troisgros
films.formalNote: Wiseman's method still intact six decades on: pure observation, structure found in the edit.

films.title: Salesman
films.formalNote: American direct cinema: presence without formal interview.

films.title: Grey Gardens
films.formalNote: Direct cinema that lets subjects address the camera conversationally without formal interviews.

films.title: Honeyland
films.formalNote: Three years of pure observation of a Macedonian beekeeper; narrative arc entirely found, never asked for.

films.title: 20,000 Days on Earth
films.formalNote: Nick Cave's day is entirely constructed: scripted drives with ghosts from his past in the passenger seat, a psychoanalyst session as the interview device. Staged frame, real disclosure.

films.title: Faces Places
films.formalNote: Encounters rather than interviews: the filmmakers' road trip and friendship is the elicitation method.

films.title: The Gleaners and I
films.formalNote: Varda's late style: conversation as gleaning.

films.title: The Beaches of Agnes
films.formalNote: Varda's self-portrait in the conversational, essayistic late style.

films.title: Grizzly Man
films.formalNote: Herzog listens on camera to the audio of Treadwell's death and refuses to let us hear it: the withheld interview as ethical statement. In narration he argues with his dead subject's worldview, a posthumous debate between two filmmakers.

films.title: Sr.
films.formalNote: Robert Downey Jr. interviews his dying father while the father simultaneously edits his own version of the film. Two films arguing with each other.

films.title: The Jinx
films.formalNote: The hot-mic bathroom monologue: the interview setting itself became evidence in a murder trial. Also the series format's capacity for a cliffhanger becoming a news event.

search.query: The Jinx: The Life and Deaths of Robert Durst

films.title: Navalny
films.formalNote: Navalny prank-calls his own would-be assassins on camera and extracts a confession in real time. The sting call as centerpiece scene.

films.title: The Mole
films.formalNote: Ten years of hidden-camera infiltration of North Korean arms dealing by a civilian. Every conversation is simultaneously an interview and an undercover operation.

search.query: The Mole: Undercover in North Korea

films.title: The Work
films.formalNote: Four days inside group therapy at Folsom Prison. No sit-down interviews; the group session's own emotional mechanics replace every conventional elicitation tool.

films.title: Man on Wire
films.formalNote: Philippe Petit's 1974 World Trade Center walk told as a heist movie. Proof that structure alone can transform archive plus interview into cinema.

films.title: The Imposter
films.formalNote: A con man narrates his own deception directly to camera, and the film lets him con the audience too before pulling the rug. Unreliable narrator as structural principle.

films.title: Icarus
films.formalNote: Starts as a self-experiment about doping, then transforms into a geopolitical thriller mid-stream. The gold standard for a documentary that changes genre while you watch.

films.title: Free Solo
films.formalNote: Engineering suspense when the outcome is publicly known, partly by making the crew's fear of filming a death part of the story.

films.title: Searching for Sugar Man
films.formalNote: Structured as a detective mystery, withholding a fact known to millions of South Africans to preserve the reveal for everyone else.

films.title: Three Identical Strangers
films.formalNote: A cascade structure: each act detonates the premise of the previous one, from feel-good reunion to ethics horror story.

films.title: Dear Zachary
films.formalNote: A memorial film whose mid-film and late-film revelations are structured for maximum devastation. The extreme case of editing as emotional engineering.

search.query: Dear Zachary: A Letter to a Son About His Father

films.title: Capturing the Friedmans
films.formalNote: Contradictory home movies and testimony arranged so certainty keeps dissolving. The structure refuses to adjudicate.

films.title: The Thin Blue Line
films.formalNote: The roadside shooting reenacted again and again, each version reflecting a different witness's claim. Repetition exposes testimony as construction, and the film freed an innocent man.

films.title: Sans Soleil
films.formalNote: Fictional letters from an invented cameraman, read by an unnamed woman, drifting across Japan, Guinea-Bissau, and memory itself. The headwater of the entire essay tradition.

films.title: F for Fake
films.formalNote: An essay about forgery that forges itself: Welles promises to tell the truth for one hour, then exploits the expiry of that promise. Editing as sleight of hand.

films.title: Cameraperson
films.formalNote: A memoir assembled entirely from outtakes of other people's films the cinematographer shot over 25 years. Structure by resonance rather than argument.

films.title: 13th
films.formalNote: The argumentative essay at full force: a single thesis prosecuted across 150 years of evidence.

films.title: Soundtrack to a Coup d'Etat
films.formalNote: The Lumumba assassination cut to the rhythms of jazz, with musicians as both soundtrack and historical actors. Montage-as-argument at a density few films attempt.

films.title: Stories We Tell
films.formalNote: A family secret investigated through competing family narrators, with archival Super 8 footage revealed late to be staged reconstruction. The reveal reframes everything you trusted.

films.title: Close-Up
films.formalNote: A real trial of a man who impersonated a filmmaker, with all participants replaying their own roles. The founding text of fiction/nonfiction hybrid structure.

films.title: Wormwood
films.formalNote: Interviews interleaved with lavish scripted drama. Docudrama not as reenactment filler but as a co-equal narrative strand.

films.title: News from Home
films.formalNote: Static shots of New York while Akerman reads her mother's increasingly plaintive letters from Belgium. Distance between image and text is the entire emotional content.

films.title: Time
films.formalNote: Two decades of a mother's home-video diaries braided non-chronologically with the present, collapsing past and present into one continuous act of waiting.

films.title: Man with a Movie Camera
films.formalNote: The city symphony and the reflexive documentary invented simultaneously, a century ago, and still formally ahead of most contemporary work.

films.title: Koyaanisqatsi
films.formalNote: Image and Philip Glass score in strict partnership; the argument about technology and speed is carried entirely by rhythm.

films.title: Samsara
films.formalNote: The 70mm globe-spanning successor; useful for studying how motif placement substitutes for narrative.

films.title: Dahomey
films.formalNote: Twenty-six looted royal artifacts return from Paris to Benin, and one of them narrates in a processed, subterranean voice. The impossible narrator reframes restitution from the object's point of view.

films.title: Aanikoobijigan
films.formalNote: Anishinaabe ancestors trapped in museum archives bend time and space to find their way home. A companion piece to Dahomey and possibly the start of a movement.

films.title: The Up Series
films.formalNote: Fourteen British children revisited every seven years for 56 years. The subjects' evolving resentment and negotiation of the project is increasingly part of the films themselves.

films.title: Hoop Dreams
films.formalNote: Five years with two Chicago basketball hopefuls; the film's honesty about class and dashed promise came from duration no shoot schedule could fake.

films.title: Quest
films.formalNote: Ten years with one North Philadelphia family, begun as a photo essay. The modest, patient model of embedding.

films.title: The Staircase
films.formalNote: Sixteen years following a murder case, complicated by the revelation that the editor fell in love with the subject during production. A landmark ethics case study about proximity.

films.title: Minding the Gap
films.formalNote: Liu films his own skateboarding friends over twelve years, then turns the camera on his own history of domestic abuse. The moment the director becomes a subject is the film's hinge.

films.title: Strong Island
films.formalNote: The director investigates the killing of his own brother and the justice system's indifference, addressing the camera in extreme close-up. Grief as method.

films.title: Dick Johnson Is Dead
films.formalNote: Johnson repeatedly stages her father's death and heaven in slapstick fantasy sequences, with his delighted participation, as a way of pre-grieving his dementia together. Collaboration as an act of love.

films.title: The Missing Picture
films.formalNote: Panh reconstructs his family's destruction under the Khmer Rouge using hand-carved clay figurines, because no images exist and none could be trusted. Subject, filmmaker, and survivor are one person.

films.title: Roger & Me
films.formalNote: The ambush quest as structure: Moore's failed attempts to meet GM's CEO organize the whole film.

films.title: Kurt & Courtney
films.formalNote: The process of failing to make the film becomes the film: boom mic in shot, phone calls on camera, doors closing.

films.title: Biggie & Tupac
films.formalNote: Broomfield's performative investigation mode: the pursuit on camera is the story.

films.title: Louis Theroux's Weird Weekends
films.formalNote: The faux-naive persona that lets subjects reveal themselves by explaining things to an apparently harmless man.

films.title: No Other Land
films.director: Basel Adra, Yuval Abraham, Hamdan Ballal, Rachel Szor
films.formalNote: A Palestinian-Israeli collective co-directs the destruction of Masafer Yatta, with the friendship and asymmetry between Adra and Abraham examined inside the film.

films.title: The Fogo Process
films.formalNote: The historical root of participatory video: 27 short films made with Fogo Island fishermen, screened back to the community and to government, with the feedback loop changing policy. A methodology to study rather than a single film.

films.title: Tickled
films.formalNote: A quirky story about competitive tickling turns into legal threats, private investigators, and the subject actively hunting the filmmakers during production. The film documents its own besiegement.

films.title: Catfish
films.formalNote: The filmmakers' relationship with their subject inverts when they discover the deception; the confrontation is handled with unexpected tenderness.

films.title: Val
films.formalNote: Built from Val Kilmer's decades of self-shot video, narrated by his son in his father's cadence. The archive is the subject's own lifelong self-documentation.

films.title: NYT Op-Docs
films.formalNote: The commissioned short as an opinion page in film form. Publishes free online. Watching ten is the fastest education in what short nonfiction can do.

films.title: Field of Vision
films.formalNote: Co-founded by Laura Poitras: the commissioned short as urgent political cinema. Publishes free online.

films.title: A Love Song for Latasha
films.formalNote: A murdered girl remembered through dreamlike reconstruction and the voices of those who loved her, refusing news-story framing entirely. What lyricism can do in under twenty minutes.

films.title: The Elephant Whisperers
films.formalNote: The Netflix-era prestige short: feature-quality cinematography compressed, Oscar winner, and a case study in the 40-minute ceiling.

films.title: 60 Second Docs
films.formalNote: The institutionalized micro-doc: one subject, one minute, hard structural formula (hook, character, turn, button). Hundreds of episodes make the formula legible fast.

films.title: O.J.: Made in America
films.formalNote: Nearly eight hours that uses length to fuse biography, city history, and race in America into one argument. Won the Oscar as a film, after which the Academy banned multi-part works.

films.title: The Last Dance
films.formalNote: The access-deal series: Jordan's production company held sway over the archive, making it the key case study in what access costs editorially.

films.title: Tie Xi Qu: West of the Tracks
films.formalNote: Nine hours on the death of a Chinese industrial district. Duration is the argument: deindustrialization cannot be understood in ninety minutes.

search.query: Tie Xi Qu: West of the Tracks

films.title: NRK Slow TV
films.formalNote: Seven unbroken hours of a train journey; 134 hours of a coastal voyage, live, watched by half of Norway. Broadcast duration as national campfire.

films.title: Bear 71
films.formalNote: A grizzly tracked by trail cameras, narrated in first person, navigated by the user through a surveillance-grid interface. Still the best argument for the interactive form.

films.title: Highrise
films.formalNote: Multi-year NFB interactive project on vertical living worldwide; also a landmark of co-creation methodology.

films.title: Welcome to Pine Point
films.formalNote: A vanished mining town remembered as an interactive scrapbook. The lesson of the category: platform dependency killed many of these works.

films.title: Clouds Over Sidra
films.formalNote: 360-degree portrait of a Syrian girl in Za'atari camp, screened to donors and credited with measurably increasing pledges. The founding case for VR as an advocacy format.

films.title: Notes on Blindness: Into Darkness
films.formalNote: Companion VR to the feature, rendering John Hull's audio diaries as spatial sound and dissolving imagery. Form matches subject: what the world is when built from hearing.

films.title: Carne y Arena
films.formalNote: Walk-through installation putting one visitor at a time inside a border crossing. Received a special Oscar; the museum piece as documentary venue.

films.title: Life in a Day
films.formalNote: Assembled from 80,000 YouTube submissions filmed on a single day worldwide. The edit finds a day-shaped arc in the planet's home footage.

films.title: Life in a Day 2020
films.formalNote: The deliberate time-capsule repeat of the 2011 open-call experiment, a decade later.

films.title: Symbiopsychotaxiplasm: Take One
films.formalNote: A film about its own making, layered crews filming each other. Possibly its own category of recursive documentary.

films.title: All Light, Everywhere
films.formalNote: Essay on cameras, surveillance, and the blind spot in vision itself. Technique and subject collapse into each other.

films.title: Austerlitz
films.formalNote: Fixed cameras silently watching tourists at concentration camp memorials. Durational observation as moral question.

films.title: Militantropos
films.formalNote: Ukraine war doc noted at Cannes for inventive cinematography. Needs watching to place.

films.title: Predators
films.formalNote: Interrogates To Catch a Predator and its vigilante descendants. Reportedly formally rigorous media-archaeology.
